sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now the bottom block (id=13) is all white again (100 pixels, back to a single block). The SPACE with white selected restored the bottom block to all white. So SPACE with a color selected stamps that color onto the bottom block, overwriting whatever was there.
Let me reconsider the goal. The legend shows:
- Top: white rectangle
- Bottom: purple rectangle
The game area currently has:
- Top: white rectangle (id=12)
- Bottom: white rectangle (id=13)
So I need to make the bottom block purple. But wait - the legend shows white ON TOP of purple. Maybe the legend IS the target pattern for a single block - meaning the bottom block should be half-white on top and half-purple on bottom.
Let me think about the sequence:
1. Select purple, press SPACE -> stamps purple on top half of bottom block (giving purple-top/white-bottom)
2. Select white, press SPACE -> stamps white, restoring the whole block to white
So SPACE stamps the selected color onto the ENTIRE bottom block, not just half. But earlier when I pressed SPACE with purple selected, it only colored the top half purple... Let me re-examine.
Actually wait - earlier when I pressed SPACE with purple selected (first time), the bottom block went from 100 white pixels to 50 purple + 50 white. But now when I pressed SPACE with white selected, it went back to 100 white pixels. So it seems like SPACE fills the bottom block with the selected color. But the first time, it only filled the top half...
Hmm, maybe SPACE fills from the top down to match the amount of the selected color in the legend. The legend white block has 50 pixels and the purple block has 50 pixels. When I selected purple and pressed SPACE, it filled 50 pixels (top half) with purple. When I selected white and pressed SPACE, it filled 100 pixels (all) with white because... hmm, that doesn't quite work.
Wait, let me re-read: the legend white block (id=5) has 50 pixels and the legend purple block (id=9) has 50 pixels. Both are 5x10 rectangles. When I selected purple and pressed SPACE, the bottom block got split: top 50 pixels purple, bottom 50 pixels white. When I selected white and pressed SPACE, the whole block became 100 pixels white.
Maybe SPACE fills the bottom block with the selected color, but only up to the pixel count of the selected legend block? Purple has 50 pixels, so it filled 50. White has 50 pixels too, so... that doesn't explain why it filled 100.
Or maybe SPACE works differently: it fills the bottom block with the selected color, but if the block already has some color from a previous stamp, it only fills the remaining area? No, that doesn't work either.
Let me try a different approach. Maybe I need to:
1. Select purple, press SPACE -> fills bottom block with purple (50 pixels from top)
2. Then select white, press SPACE -> fills remaining with white
But that gave me purple-top/white-bottom, which is the REVERSE of the legend (white-top/purple-bottom).
Actually, maybe I need to do it in the opposite order:
1. First select white and press SPACE to fill from top
2. Then select purple and press SPACE to fill from bottom
But when I tried white first, it filled the entire block. Unless... the order matters. Let me try: first make the bottom block all white (it already is), then select purple and press SPACE.
Actually wait - the bottom block IS all white now. Let me select purple and press SPACE.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current state: bottom block is all white (id=13, 100 pixels)
# Let me select purple and press SPACE to stamp purple
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 4, 'col': 43}])  # click purple selector box
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After selecting purple:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['id'] in [8, 12, 13]:
        print(f"  id={n['id']} color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Now press SPACE
action(['SPACE'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nAfter SPACE with purple:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['id'] in [8, 12, 13]:
        print(f"  id={n['id']} color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} boundary={n['boundary']}")
print("Result:", last_action_result)
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
